text:
My house was built on a foundation of wood. It works well normally because I put a whole lot of wood under it. 

But termites came and started to eat the wood. Slowly but surely, the wood got thinned out. My house still was fine. Everybody was still living inside comfortably. I ignored the termites since nobody was complaining and didn't add more wood back. The new narrative was a glut of wood anyway, don't worry.

Suddenly, a big storm came. My shaky wooden foundation crumbled. All hell broke loose. But my neighbour next door with cement foundation was fine. 

How I wish I fixed that termite issue when I had the time.

IYKYK.

#oott

Mexico hedges a significant chunk of its oil exports every year, typically in the Jul-Aug-Sept period in a massive program known as the "Hacienda" hedge. 

I claim no knowledge of what sovereign hedgers are doing, but clearly current oil prices are quite inviting. Just saying.

CHART OF THE DAY: Morgan Stanley reckons that more than half of Russian oil refinery capacity is down due to Ukrainian attacks. 

Typically, Russia is the world's second-largest diesel exporter - normally around 11% of global seaborne trade. https://t.co/q8yjgcTHVF

Another ugly night in the Strait of Hormuz, with at least one tanker hit and on fire (according to @UK_MTO warning). The Greek shipowners (plus a few others) are still crossing the strait, at great risk for crews and ships.

U.S. RIGS drilling for oil have climbed to an average of 447 so far in July, the highest for fourteen months, and up from a recent low of just 409 in February, before the war with Iran lifted oil prices. The extra drilling is likely to filter through into higher output during the first or second quarters of 2027:

Now Ukraine is going after crude loading. 

First they strike the refineries, now the tankers, and then the export terminals. 

At some point, export capacity will jam into onshore storage, which will jam production forcing Russia to shut-in production. 

That’s not far away.

The oil market has already gone through 2 iterations of this, but I’ll just say it again. 

The conflict over the Strait of Hormuz won’t have an in-between solution. Either Iran controls it or it doesn’t. 

The US cannot allow Iran to control it, toll it, or whatever because it would make it the most powerful oil producer in the world. 

In the near term, prices will continue to trade off of headlines, but when the reality that this is an all or nothing sets in, it will be too late.

Russia's Foreign Ministry on Monday accused Ukraine of attacking oil tankers during loading at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium terminal at Novorossiysk, and said that Kyiv was trying to destabilise global oil markets.

The CPC, which exports oil mainly from Kazakhstan, said on Monday that it had again suspended loadings after a brief resumption, following a new Ukrainian drone attack on a tanker.

Confirmed oil flow through the Strait of Hormuz has collapsed to ~4 MMbpd on a trailing 10-day average basis.

Down from ~15 MMbpd in late-June and at the lowest level since late-May. https://t.co/X4P4jTlbqi

Iran's FM Spokesperson Esmail Baghaei:

Iran is determined, in cooperation with Oman, to adopt the necessary measures and arrangements to fully exercise its sovereignty and prevent any security threats in the Strait of Hormuz. https://t.co/Ox86ScmjiL
